questions.question: Explain the concept of file system mounting and why it is essential in operating systems.
keyPoints: Mounting integrates a file system into the OS directory tree at a mount point
Enables access to storage devices and their contents through standard file operations
Without mounting, each storage device would exist in isolation from the unified file hierarchy
Supports hot-plugging and dynamic addition of storage resources

questions.question: What is a mount point? Describe the requirements for a valid mount point.
keyPoints: A mount point is an existing directory in the file system hierarchy
Typically an empty directory but can contain files hidden by the mount
In Unix: /mnt, /media, /home are common mount points
In Windows: drive letters (C:, D:) or folder mount points
Must have appropriate permissions for access

questions.question: Describe the role of Virtual File System (VFS) in mounting operations.
keyPoints: VFS provides an abstraction layer between OS and actual file systems
Enables uniform interface for different file system types (NTFS, ext4, FAT32)
Handles translation of generic operations to file-system-specific operations
Allows simultaneous mounting of multiple file system types

questions.question: Explain the difference between automatic mounting and manual mounting.
keyPoints: Automatic mounting happens at system boot or device hot-plug without user intervention
Configured through /etc/fstab in Unix or Windows auto-mount settings
Manual mounting requires explicit user command (mount command)
Automatic mounting uses predefined options; manual allows custom options each time

questions.question: List and explain three common mount options used in Unix systems.
keyPoints: ro (read-only): Mounts file system without write permissions
rw (read-write): Allows read and write operations (default)
noexec: Prevents execution of binaries on that file system (security)
nosuid: Ignores setuid and setgid bits on files
sync: Forces synchronous I/O operations for data integrity

questions.question: What happens during the mount operation? Describe the steps involved.
keyPoints: OS validates the file system by reading superblock
Allocates kernel data structures for the mount
Verifies file system type and checks for errors
Updates the mount table with new mount entry
Creates VFS node and makes file system accessible at mount point

questions.question: What is the purpose of unmounting? Explain why it is important to unmount before removing a device.
keyPoints: Unmounting detaches the file system from the directory tree
Ensures all buffered writes are flushed to disk
Closes all file handles and releases system resources
Prevents data loss or file system corruption
Allows safe physical removal of the storage device

questions.question: Describe the file system mounting process in Unix/Linux systems. Include discussion of mount tables, configuration files, and the kernel data structures involved.
keyPoints: /etc/fstab contains static mount configuration: device, mount point, type, options
At boot, init process reads fstab and mounts defined file systems
Kernel maintains mount table (struct vfsmount) for each mounted filesystem
/proc/mounts or /etc/mtab shows currently mounted filesystems
Mount syscall takes device and directory as arguments
VFS layer creates mount point inode and associates file system operations
Each mount creates a vfsmount structure with superblock and mount flags
Umount removes entry from mount table after flushing buffers

questions.question: Explain network file system mounting. How does it differ from local mounting? Discuss NFS as an example.
keyPoints: Network mounting allows mounting remote file systems over a network
Remote files appear as local directories through mount point
NFS (Network File System) is Unix standard; SMB/CIFS is Windows equivalent
Differences: network latency, dependency on network connectivity, security concerns
Mount uses remote server IP and export path: mount server:/share /mnt/remote
Client relies on RPC for file operations across network
Caching strategies differ due to network considerations
Disconnection handling is more complex than local mounts

questions.question: Compare file system mounting in Unix/Linux and Windows operating systems.
keyPoints: Unix uses directory-based mount points; Windows uses drive letters and folder mounts
Unix configuration in /etc/fstab; Windows in Registry or Disk Management
Unix mount is typically command-line; Windows has GUI Disk Management
Windows mount points called 'volume mount points' since Windows 2000
Both support automatic and manual mounting
Unix: multiple file systems unified under single tree; Windows: separate drive letters
Unix VFS provides abstraction; Windows uses Installable File System (IFS)
Unmount in Unix: umount command; Windows: dismount or eject

questions.question: Discuss bind mounting and loop device mounting. Explain their purposes and use cases.
keyPoints: Bind mounting: maps a directory or file to another location in same filesystem
Command: mount --bind /source /target or mount -o bind /source /target
Use cases: containerization, development environments, file sharing without copying
Loop device mounting: treats disk image files as block devices
Creates /dev/loop0, /dev/loop1 etc. mapping to image files
Allows mounting ISO images, disk images without burning to physical media
Useful for software installation, OS virtualization, backup verification
Both are special mount types that extend file system flexibility
